perturbation.original_text: 1.1 TRANSPORTATION QUANTITY - shall mean the maximum daily quantity of gas which Transporter agrees to receive and transport on a firm basis, subject to Article II herein, for the account of Shipper hereunder on each day during each year during the term hereof, which shall be 51,000 dekatherms.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 TRANSPORTATION QUANTITY - shall mean the estimated daily quantity of gas which Transporter agrees to receive and transport, subject to Article II herein, for the account of Shipper hereunder on each day during each year during the term hereof, which shall be 51,000 dekatherms.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'maximum daily quantity of gas which Transporter agrees to receive and transport on a firm basis' to 'estimated daily quantity of gas which Transporter agrees to receive and transport' introduces uncertainty about the firm commitment of the transporter. This could contradict regulations requiring firm transportation service to be guaranteed.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 284.8(b)(1) relates to establishing conditions for providing firm transportation service. The modification from 'maximum daily quantity' to 'estimated daily quantity' undermines the firmness requirement of the transportation service, as the 'estimated' quantity does not guarantee a fixed quantity, potentially violating the regulation's intent to provide firm, guaranteed transportation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 284.8 - Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 284.8 - Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 284.8 Release of firm capacity on interstate pipelines. (a) An interstate pipeline that offers transportation service on a firm basis under subpart B or G of this part must include in its tariff a mechanism for firm shippers to release firm capacity to the pipeline for resale by the pipeline on a firm basis under this section. (b) (1) Firm shippers must be permitted to release their capacity, in whole or in part, on a permanent or short-term basis, without restriction on the terms or conditions of the release. A firm shipper may arrange for a replacement shipper to obtain its released capacity from the pipeline. A replacement shipper is any shipper that obtains released capacity. (2) The rate charged the replacement shipper for a release of capacity may not exceed the applicable maximum rate, except that no rate limitation applies to the release of capacity for a period of one year or less if the release is to take effect on or before one year from the date on which the pipeline is notified of the release. Payments or other consideration exchanged between the releasing and replacement shippers in a release to an asset manager as defined in paragraph (h)(3) of this section are not subject to the maximum rate. (c) Except as provided in paragraph (h) of this section, a firm shipper that wants to release any or all of its firm capacity must notify the pipeline of the terms and conditions under which the shipper will release its capacity. The firm shipper must also notify the pipeline of any replacement shipper designated to obtain the released capacity under the terms and conditions specified by the firm shipper. (d) The pipeline must provide notice of offers to release or

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'maximum daily quantity' to 'estimated daily quantity' weakens the firmness requirement for gas transportation, potentially contradicting the intent of regulations aimed at ensuring firm, guaranteed transportation service. While the provided law snippets do not explicitly define 'firm transportation service' or the exact parameters for establishing conditions for providing it, Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 CFR § 284.8) indicates that interstate pipelines offering transportation service on a 'firm basis' must include in their tariff a mechanism for firm shippers to release firm capacity. The change undermines the commitment implied by 'firm basis' by introducing estimation, making it less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE VI - RATES AND CHARGES FOR GAS TRANSPORTATION 6.1 TRANSPORTATION RATES - Commencing upon the effective date hereof, the rates, charges, and surcharges to be paid by Shipper to Transporter for the transportation service provided herein shall be in accordance with transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE VI - RATES AND CHARGES FOR GAS TRANSPORTATION 6.1 TRANSPORTATION RATES - Commencing upon the effective date hereof, the rates, charges, and surcharges to be paid by Shipper to Transporter for the transportation service provided herein shall be solely determined by Transporter's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the requirement to follow 'transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and the General Terms and Conditions of Transporter's FERC Gas Tariff' with 'solely determined by Transporter's discretion' allows the transporter to set rates arbitrarily, potentially violating FERC regulations for just and reasonable rates.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 717c requires that all rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared unlawful. Giving the transporter sole discretion over rates contradicts this requirement for just and reasonable rates as determined by FERC regulations, potentially leading to discriminatory or excessive charges.
perturbation.location: Article VI - Rates and Charges for Gas Transportation, 6.1

Rates and charges (a) Just and reasonable rates and charges All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission, and all rules and regulations affecting or pertaining to such rates or charges, shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful. (b) Undue preferences and unreasonab
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 717c, which mandates that all rates and charges for natural gas transportation must be just and reasonable. By granting the transporter sole discretion over rates, the changed text removes the requirement for rates to be determined by the existing Rate Schedule FT-A and FERC Gas Tariff, as stated in the original text. This directly undermines the 'just and reasonable' standard mandated by federal law, specifically 15 USC 717c (a): 'All rates and charges made, demanded, or received by any natural-gas company for or in connection with the transportation or sale of natural gas...shall be just and reasonable, and any such rate or charge that is not just and reasonable is declared to be unlawful.'

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE VIII - GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS This Agreement shall be subject to the effective provisions of Transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and to the General Terms and Conditions incorporated therein, as the same may be changed or superseded from time to time in accordance with the rules and regulations of the FERC.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE VIII - GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS This Agreement shall not be subject to the effective provisions of Transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and to the General Terms and Conditions incorporated therein, as the same may be changed or superseded from time to time in accordance with the rules and regulations of the FERC.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the subjection to 'Transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and to the General Terms and Conditions incorporated therein' allows the contract to operate outside of FERC regulation, which dictates standards of conduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 CFR § 154.1 requires that tariff filings must conform to the requirements of this part. By stating that the agreement is not subject to the Transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and General Terms and Conditions, the agreement is attempting to operate outside of the regulations established by FERC.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE VIII - GENERAL TERMS AND CONDITIONS
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 154.1 - Application; Obligation to file. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 154.1 - Application; Obligation to file. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 154.1 Application; Obligation to file. (a) The provisions of this part apply to filings pursuant to section 4 of the Natural Gas Act . (b) Every natural gas company must file with the Commission and post in conformity with the requirements of this part, schedules showing all rates and charges for any transportation or sale of natural gas subject to the jurisdiction of the Commission , and the classifications, practices, rules, and regulations affecting such rates, charges, and services, together with all contracts related thereto. (c) No natural gas company may file, under this part, any new or changed rate schedule or contract for the performance of any service for which a certificate of public convenience and necessity or certificate amendment must be obtained pursuant to section 7(c) of the Natural Gas Act , until such certificate has been issued. (d) For the purposes of paragraph (b) of this section, any contract that conforms to the form of service agreement that is part of the pipeline's tariff pursuant to § 154.110 does not have to be filed. Any contract or executed service agreement which deviates in any material aspect from the form of service agreement in the tariff is subject to the filing requirements of this part. perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly states that the agreement shall *not* be subject to the Transporter's Rate Schedule FT-A and its General Terms and Conditions, which directly contradicts the requirement in 18 CFR § 154.1(b) that every natural gas company must file schedules showing all rates, charges, classifications, practices, rules, and regulations affecting such rates, charges, and services, along with all related contracts. By removing the subjection to the Rate Schedule and General Terms, the agreement attempts to operate outside of FERC regulations, undermining the obligation to file and conform to those regulations as stated in the law snippet.
